data.title: Payward raises $200 million from 17 projects that have raised $443 million.
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

This week, a total of 17 blockchain startups completed funding rounds, raising a total of $443 million.

Largest Funding Round This Week

Payward raised $200 million from Deutsche Börse Group.

Payward is the parent company of Kraken and its related products, and its business covers crypto trading, custody, payments, lending, on-chain finance, and benchmark services. Its positioning is to provide infrastructure for an open, programmable global market, connecting crypto assets, tokenized assets, and traditional finance through a unified, interoperable technology stack.

The company states that its business covers 190+ jurisdictions and holds 100+ licenses and registrations, encompassing crypto, securities, derivatives, payments, remittances, banking, and lending. Claw Intelligence has completed a $3 million seed funding round, with investors including Castrum Capital, Titans Ventures, Super Labs, and Genesis Capital VC. Claw Intelligence is an AI-powered Web3 intelligence and trading platform focused on the BNB Chain. It integrates market data, on-chain signals, research analysis, and execution tools into a single interface, aiming to transform fragmented blockchain information into structured, actionable insights. Its chat-based and modular design allows users to more efficiently transition from analysis to trade execution. KieDex Raises $3.5 Million Seed Funding. KieDex is a crypto futures trading platform focused on the high-leverage perpetual contract market, employing a "trade-to-earn" model. Positioned as a fast and simple exchange, users can start trading with free USDT and earn KDX rewards through trading activities. The platform emphasizes speed, fairness, and long-term incentives, leaning more towards derivatives DeFi trading than general-purpose exchanges or infrastructure protocols. Mirage Raises Seed Funding (Amount Undisclosed). Mirage is a non-custodial privacy protocol for stablecoin transfers on Ethereum and other EVM-compatible networks. It enables privacy payments similar to regular on-chain transactions without pooling funds. Mirage focuses on fast settlement, compliant integration, and easy embedding into wallets, payment applications, custody services, and DeFi products. Its core goal is to achieve low-friction, high-availability, and broadly compatible "privacy digital dollar transfers." Nava Labs raised $8.3 million in seed funding. Nava is a middleware verification layer for AI Agent blockchain transactions. Before funds are transferred, it verifies that the Agent's on-chain execution conforms to its original instructions. Through arbitration verification, execution custody, and a dedicated coordination layer (Nava Chain), the system performs security, correctness, and policy compliance analysis on transactions. Its goal is to make AI-automated DeFi operations more reliable, auditable, and financially secure, making it more suitable for institutional scenarios. Pumpcade raised $5 million in seed funding. Pumpcade is a Solana-based platform for instantly creating and trading meme tokens. It simplifies the token issuance process, allowing users to quickly publish and list assets without complex technical skills. The platform is community-driven, promoting participation, discovery, and viral spread within the meme token ecosystem. SHARE has completed its seed round of funding (amount undisclosed). Share is a decentralized internet infrastructure network (DePIN) focused on solving connectivity issues in Africa and emerging markets. Through a distributed node network, it reduces fragmentation, high costs, and dependence on centralized service providers. The platform provides open, reliable, and low-cost internet access and data services. Its goal is to improve accessibility and drive the growth of the digital economy in underdeveloped regions. Other Funding Rounds Mindra AI completed a strategic funding round (amount undisclosed). Mindra AI is a decentralized Large Model (LLM) infrastructure network that builds a "Intelligence Grid." It supports dynamic inference routing, cross-chain agent collaboration, and introduces AI-Fi to financialize computing resources. Through DeAI technology, it enhances network intelligence, scalability, and user experience while reducing reliance on centralized AI providers. Spektr completed a $20 million Series A funding round. Spektr is an AI-driven compliance operations platform that helps banks, fintech, and Web3 companies automate KYC, KYB, customer onboarding, monitoring, remediation, document review, ownership structure analysis, and risk assessment. It combines configurable processes with AI agents, enabling teams to scale compliance capabilities and reduce manual costs. Slash has completed a $100 million Series C funding round. Slash is a vertically integrated digital banking platform built specifically for online businesses. It offers checking accounts, virtual/physical cards (with cashback), transfer services, and integration with mainstream accounting software. It supports multi-user, real-time account partitioning, and seamless management of crypto and fiat currencies, serving e-commerce, marketing, and crypto-native enterprises. ZenGo was acquired by eToro for $70 million. ZenGo is a self-custodied crypto wallet that uses MPC technology to eliminate mnemonic phrase risk. It supports 380+ assets and provides 3D facial recognition and triple authentication recovery mechanisms. Brix raised $5.5 million. Brix is ​​a DeFi platform that brings institutional-grade strategies on-chain through structured yield products. Its first asset, iTRY, is a token based on the Turkish Lira Money Market Fund, allowing users to participate in arbitrage trading that was previously limited to banks. BlockInvest raised $4.7 million. BlockInvest is a Web3 investment and asset management platform for financial institutions. It leverages blockchain and AI to tokenize assets and improve data quality and liquidity. Totalis raised $500,000. Totalis is an on-chain prediction market derivatives layer that supports the construction of parlays. It covers multiple sectors including geopolitics, crypto, and sports. Paxos Labs (Amplify) raised $12 million. Paxos Labs is an enterprise-grade on-chain financial platform incubated by Paxos. Its core product, Amplify, provides a unified SDK that supports the integration of yield, lending, and stablecoin issuance functions. OpenGradient raised $9.5 million in funding. OpenGradient is a decentralized AI model deployment and execution platform. It provides NeuroML (similar to the Solidity framework) and a Python SDK for connecting to model libraries and infrastructure. Fluent Labs raised $1 million in public funding. Fluent is an Ethereum Layer 2 network and development framework that integrates EVM, SVM, and Wasm into a unified execution environment. This "hybrid execution" supports seamless interoperability between smart contracts in different languages ​​(Solidity, Rust, etc.).

▌Justin Sun Redeemed 53,665 ETH from Aave 5 Hours Ago

On April 19th, according to on-chain analyst Ai Yi, Justin Sun redeemed 53,665 ETH from Aave 5 hours ago, worth $126 million. This portion of ETH is currently still in his wallet and has not yet been transferred.

▌KelpDAO Hacked, Losses Approximately $292 Million

On April 19th, attackers used KelpDAO's LayerZero-based bridging protocol to steal 116,500 rsETH, worth approximately $292 million, representing about 18% of the token's circulating supply, triggering an emergency suspension of the core contract.

Because the bridging protocol held reserves backing rsETH on more than 20 networks, this loss raised questions about rsETH support on Layer 2 networks and triggered a wave of freezes on protocols including Aave, SparkLend, and Fluid. This vulnerability became the largest DeFi hack of 2026, occurring against the backdrop of a surge in DeFi attacks and putting immense pressure on rsETH's pegging mechanism and KelpDAO's redemption capabilities. On April 18th, RaveDAO responded to recent fluctuations in the RAVE token price on the X platform, explicitly stating that the team did not participate in market manipulation and did not influence price fluctuations. RaveDAO stated that in the future, it will explore token lock-up mechanisms, including those triggered by price or performance, to link team incentives with ecosystem growth. The team also plans to sell a portion of unlocked tokens at an appropriate time according to the established TRS mechanism for expenses such as operations, recruitment, marketing, and strategic investments. RaveDAO emphasized that the project's long-term goal is to promote the large-scale adoption of Web3 through offline activities and has committed to using a portion of the proceeds for charitable purposes. Currently, 20% of the activity profits have been used for charity. Going forward, it will continue to focus on product, community, and compliance building, promoting the integration of on-chain entertainment with the real world.

Ethereum co-founder Lubin: AI Will Be a Key Turning Point for the Next Stage of Crypto Industry Development, But Beware of Tech Giant Monopolies. On April 18th, Ethereum co-founder Joseph Lubin stated that artificial intelligence will be a key turning point for the next stage of development in the crypto industry, but if computing power and infrastructure are controlled by a few large technology companies, it could bring systemic risks. In the future, AI agents can autonomously execute transactions, collaborate, and verify on blockchain networks, becoming the foundation of the "machine economy." Cryptographic technology and decentralized systems will ensure transparency and accountability mechanisms, enabling machines to supervise each other. Furthermore, he believes that traditional finance and DeFi are accelerating their integration, driving the global economy towards a more refined and programmable direction. The risks of quantum computing are a long-term manageable issue, and Ethereum developers have already taken precautions. Cryptocurrency

On April 19th, Bitwise CIO: Increased Global Currency Uncertainty is Boosting BTC's Status, $1.3 Million Target Price by 2035 May Be Too Conservative

On April 19th, Bitwise Chief Investment Officer Matt Hougan believes that increased global currency uncertainty is boosting Bitcoin's status, and his target price of $1.3 million by 2035 may actually be too conservative.

▌ Pump.fun's Official Instagram Account Hacked, Please Be Vigilant

On April 19th, according to SolanaFloor, Pump.fun's official Instagram account has been hacked and is currently being used to issue and promote Meme coin, likely with the intention of tricking users into clicking malicious links. The account has been under hacker control for the past two hours—please be vigilant.

...

▌ether.fi: Tracking the Kelp rsETH situation and will take all necessary measures

On April 19th, ether.fi posted on the X platform, stating, "We are closely tracking the Kelp rsETH situation and coordinating closely with our security partners to ensure that weETH is not affected in any way. We will take all necessary measures based on the advice of our security partners. More details will be released as soon as possible."

▌KelpDAO hackers stole rsETH and deposited it into multiple lending protocols, lending out a large amount of WETH

On April 19th, according to PeckShield monitoring, today, the hackers involved in the KelpDAO attack deposited the stolen rsETH into multiple lending protocols (AaveV3, Compound, etc.).

V3, Euler), and lent out a large amount of WETH, with related debts exceeding $236 million. Sonic: Sonic Network and Aave Deployment on Sonic Unaffected by Kelp Incident, User Funds Safe. On April 19th, Sonic issued a statement on its X platform stating, "The Sonic Network, assets held by Sonic Labs, and Aave deployment on Sonic are unrelated to today's Kelp incident. Aave contracts were unaffected, and user funds on Sonic remain safe. Our team will continue to closely monitor the situation." Important Economic Developments: Data: With Iran closing the Strait of Hormuz again, the probability of US oil prices surging back above $100 per barrel this month rises to 44%. On April 19th, according to The Kobeissi... Letter reports that with Iran closing the Strait of Hormuz again, the probability of US oil prices surging back above $100 per barrel this month has increased to 44%. The US military claims it has "completely cut off" Iran's economic and trade activities via sea. On April 18th local time, according to the US Central Command, the USS Pinckney guided-missile destroyer was patrolling the area in support of the maritime blockade against Iran, stating that the blockade had "completely cut off" Iran's economic and trade activities via sea. (Zhitong Finance) Shipping in the Strait of Hormuz comes to a standstill again. On April 19th, according to British sources, shipping in the Strait of Hormuz came to a standstill again on the evening of the 18th. Previously, radio communications warned that Iranian armed forces had resumed "strict management and control" of the Strait of Hormuz. Multiple vessels in the area heard the warnings, which have been confirmed. On the same day, British maritime security firm Pioneer Technologies released a report detailing attacks on three vessels near the Strait of Hormuz that day: an oil tanker, a cruise ship, and a container ship. Two of the vessels were explicitly warned and fired upon by the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, while the other container ship was hit by an unidentified projectile 25 nautical miles northeast of Oman, damaging some of its containers. (CCTV) “We do not trust the enemy. Even if we sit here right now, war could break out—the armed forces are fully prepared for the battlefield,” Ghalibaf said in a speech broadcast on Iranian state television Sunday morning. While Iran is actively negotiating with the United States for a permanent ceasefire agreement, he stated that Iran is prepared to respond at any time if the enemy “makes a mistake.”

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called “Digital Gold” During Geopolitical Crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of “digital gold” has never been supported by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-prone asset," capable of functioning effectively when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Encrypted apps are being removed en masse: Google announces the end of the era of unlicensed, unprotected apps.
data.description:
Recently, many exchanges, wallets, and payment projects in the industry have been discussing the same situation:

They anxiously sent the same screenshot of their backend (see below), and then asked almost the same question: "Attorney Shao, we suddenly received app removal warnings from app stores in several countries. Is this due to competitors, or is it a targeted attack by regulators?"

Frankly, neither. Don't be paranoid, and don't think this is just an "isolated incident." If you've also received this "final warning," or if your team is still complacent about its rapid overseas expansion, please recognize this harsh reality: Behind this is the fact that global app stores are being forced to become "regulatory assistants." In the past, regulators only controlled your cash flow and office location; now, they are directly cutting off your traffic sources. This isn't targeting any one company, but a global purge of app stores. Google Play has officially included "crypto licenses" in its listing rules. In fact, Google Play already included "crypto licenses" in its "Cryptocurrency Exchange and Software Wallet Policy." The core logic is extremely simple and brutal: You must provide a license for the country where your app provides trading or custody services. Can't produce one? Sorry, it will be removed from the app store upon expiration. Google has explicitly listed the requirements for several jurisdictions, such as: the EU requires CASP authorization under MiCA granted by the relevant national competent authority; the UK requires FCA registration; the UAE requires FSRA, VARA, or DFSA licenses; the US requires registration as a FinCEN MSB and obtaining state-level money transmitter qualification, or having federal/state chartered bank status; South Korea requires successfully submitting a VASP report to KoFIU… In the past, you might only have needed to deal with regulatory agencies, but now you also have to deal with Google's compliance review team. This wave of app removals is not a punishment, but an “entry blockade.” Many Web3 founders are still stuck in the mindset of the last cycle: “So what if I don’t have a license? At worst, I won’t set up an office locally, how can the regulators check me?” Brother, times have changed. While regulators might not be able to trace your app across borders for a while, a single email from Google can cripple your growth engine. This is especially true in emerging markets like Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Latin America, where Android users are dominant. What does being removed from Google Play mean? New users won't be able to find or download it. Existing users can't update, leading to a deteriorating user experience. Expensive advertising conversion rates will drop to zero. Most critically, once your partner banks and payment gateways see your app is gone, their risk control alarms will sound, and they'll immediately terminate your partnerships. Regulatory penalties are a matter of law and fines, but having an app's internet connection cut off is a matter of life and death, directly severing its cash flow. Why the sudden crackdown? It's simple: because the global regulatory framework is complete, and platforms are no longer willing to take the blame for you. Previously, you could argue that "there's no licensing mechanism in the local area." Now? Global regulatory rules like Hong Kong's VATP, Europe's MiCA, and Singapore's PSA are almost all in place. The path has been paved for you; if you don't get the license, don't blame the platform for being uncooperative. Even Google and Apple are afraid of being dragged down. How many money laundering tools, scam wallets, and collapsed exchanges have appeared on app stores in the past few years? The platforms' attitude is now clear: Prove you're legitimate first, then I'll give you traffic. The old gray growth path of "launching globally first, building up user base first, and then fixing things when regulators come knocking" is completely unworkable. It's not just exchanges that are being targeted. Don't think you're safe just because you're not a CEX (centralized exchange). Based on recent cases we've encountered, custodial wallets, OTC deposit and withdrawal platforms, U-card projects, even if you're just making an app with an Earn feature, or the recently popular RWA platform, payment aggregators, etc., as long as your application involves "token custody" and "financial attributes," are all within the scope of this crackdown. In the next year or two, the industry will become extremely polarized. Licensed, legitimate players can openly acquire traffic and connect to bank channels; while unlicensed, guerrilla-style operators can only launch and delist daily, playing a cat-and-mouse game with regulators, and will eventually be slowly worn down. The Solution: Not Remedying After Being Taken Down, but Preparing for Licensing in Advance. At this point, anxiety won't solve the problem. There seem to be two paths on the table, but in reality, there's only one. Some say: "At worst, I'll just stop operating in those dozens of countries and remove those regions." This is called the "ostrich policy." In today's world where traffic dividends have been exhausted, actively abandoning any mainstream market is tantamount to handing over hard-won territory to competitors who have already laid the groundwork for compliance. This is a form of slow suicide. There is only one path: confront it head-on and achieve full compliance. Apply for the corresponding compliance licenses in the relevant countries/regions. This is the only long-term solution that allows you to stay on the table. But problems arise: the regulatory requirements of dozens of countries and various legal systems around the world vary greatly. Applying for a FinCEN MSB in the US, obtaining CASP certification under the MiCA framework in Europe, and facing stringent scrutiny in Hong Kong… For most Web3 projects lacking a large multinational legal team, this is practically an impossible task. This is why more and more peers, recently facing delisting warnings and forced into a corner, are turning to Mankiw. Applying for a global crypto license is never a simple matter of "filling out forms and translating." It's incredibly complex: Which country is the fastest to obtain a license for your business model? How do you get your UBO funding verification approved? How do you appoint local directors and anti-money laundering officers? Even a one-click exchange function in your app might trigger the red lines for another license? These practical issues won't be taught on the official website, and even general intermediaries don't understand them.

data.title: Canaan Creative increases its holdings by 15 BTC; Ethereum Foundation's Josh Stark resigns.
data.description:
Bitcoin

Arkham: Morgan Stanley Bitcoin ETF On-Chain Address Now Holds 1348 BTC

Blockchain analytics platform Arkham announced that it has identified the on-chain wallet address of Morgan Stanley Bitcoin Trust (MSBT), becoming the first platform to publicly identify the ETF's on-chain BTC holdings, allowing users to track fund inflows and outflows in real time. The Morgan Stanley Bitcoin ETF was listed on the NYSE Arca on April 8th, with Coinbase and BNY Mellon serving as custodians. According to Arkham's tracking data, it currently holds 1348 BTC, worth approximately $103.92 million.

ETH Zurich Foundation Core Member Josh Stark Announces Resignation

On April 17th, Josh Stark, a core figure at the Ethereum Foundation (EF), announced his resignation after five years in office, officially stepping down at the end of the month. Stark joined the Ethereum Foundation in 2019, initially working on a special projects team before rising to leadership positions. He worked with EF Chairman Aya Miyaguchi, Ethereum founder Vitalik Buterin, and co-executive directors Hsiao-Wei Wang and Bastian Aue, among other senior figures. He was one of the most prominent members of the Foundation, leading several major developments in Ethereum, including "The Merge," the upgrade from Proof-of-Work to Proof-of-Stake, and subsequent upgrades such as Dencun, Fusaka, and Pectra. During a leadership reshuffle at the Foundation last year, he was appointed as "Co-Manager" of the EF Board of Directors and recently became co-chair of the "Trillion-Dollar Security" initiative.

... Last month, Stark co-authored an Ethereum strategy blog post with Josh Rudolf and Julian Ma, outlining Ethereum's latest scaling direction and its relationship with the Layer 2 ecosystem. Stark stated, "I haven't planned for the future yet; I just want to rest and spend time with family and friends." This departure comes against the backdrop of ongoing changes within the Ethereum Foundation. Last year, the Foundation underwent a major leadership reshuffle, subsequently refocusing its strategic emphasis on Ethereum mainnet scaling and core cypherpunk values. Tomasz K. Stańczak also resigned as co-executive director at the end of February this year, after serving less than a year. On the same day, Trent Van Epps also announced his departure from EF to dedicate himself full-time to Protocol Guild, the independent funding organization for Ethereum core developers that he founded.

ETH/BTC ratio rebounds from 2026 lows, signaling an overall recovery in the crypto market

On April 15th, Ethereum recently strengthened relative to Bitcoin, with the ETH/BTC exchange rate rising to approximately 0.0313, the highest level in three months, but still far below the January peak. This improvement in the ratio is attributed to strong on-chain activity on Ethereum: a surge of 82% in new users in the quarter, record-high transaction volume, and a record-high total stablecoin supply of $180 billion. Analysts say that Ethereum must recover the 0.035 ETH/BTC level on the weekly close to indicate that the rotation of funds into Ethereum is sustainable, rather than a short-lived rebound; especially since the current Ethereum price is still more than 50% below its 52-week high.

BitMine increased its holdings by over 71,000 ETH last week, bringing its total holdings to over 4% of the total ETH supply. As of April 12th, Eastern Time, BitMine's total cryptocurrency, cash, and "Moonshot" holdings totaled $11.8 billion. BitMine held 4,874,858 ETH (an increase of 71,524 ETH from the previous week), representing 4.04% of the total ETH supply (120.7 million ETH). It also held 197 BTC, $200 million worth of Beast Industries shares, $85 million worth of Eightco Holdings (NASDAQ: ORBS), and $719 million in uncollateralized cash. As of April 13th, 2026, BitMine's total staked ETH was 3,334,637 ETH (equivalent to $7.4 billion at $2,206 per ETH). Other Projects

Singapore's Gulf Bank Launches Stablecoin Minting and Redemption Service

On April 17th, Singapore's Gulf Bank (SGB) announced the launch of a stablecoin minting and redemption service. Corporate and high-net-worth clients can directly convert between fiat currency and stablecoins through their SGB accounts, achieving 24/7 instant settlement without relying on traditional correspondent banking processes. This service is integrated into SGB's proprietary clearing network, SGB Net, supporting seamless on-chain and off-chain fund flows. Initially supporting USD Coin (USDC) with a minimum transaction amount of US$100,000, USDT, USDe (Ethena), and Global Dollar (USDG) will be added later.

... Polymarket Adds "Claude Developer Anthropic's Next Funding Round Deadline" On April 18th, Polymarket added a new event regarding the "Claude developer Anthropic's next funding round deadline." Currently, the probability of the funding round being completed by the end of June is tentatively reported as 22%, and by the end of December as 81%. The event's contractual rules state that if the designated company publicly and formally announces the completion of its next funding round by the specified date (Eastern Time), the market will be classified as "Yes." Otherwise, the market will be classified as "No." A qualifying announcement must clearly confirm the completion of the new funding round, which can be achieved through official announcements from the designated company (such as press releases) or its investors, regulatory documents, or consensus from credible media reports. Informal announcements, statements from anonymous sources, or leaks are not acceptable. If the designated company is unable to complete the new funding round due to acquisition, merger, or absorption by another entity, the market will be classified as "No." The primary source of settlement information for this market will be official announcements from designated companies, as well as official company documents such as SEC filings; however, credible consensus reports may also be used.

Payward has agreed to acquire crypto derivatives company Bitnomial for $550 million

Kraken's parent company, Payward, announced that it has agreed to acquire stock and crypto derivatives trading company Bitnomial for $550 million in a cash-and-stock deal. This transaction allows Payward to control a fully licensed U.S. cryptocurrency derivatives stack, thereby accelerating its expansion in regulated markets.

... Bitcoin mining company TeraWulf completes approximately $1.035 billion rights issue. Nasdaq-listed Bitcoin mining company TeraWulf announced the completion of its previously announced public offering of 54,510,000 ordinary shares at $19.00 per share, raising a total of $1,035,690,000. Morgan Stanley served as the lead underwriter for the offering, with Bank of America Securities, Citigroup, TD Cowen, and Wells Fargo Securities serving as joint underwriters. Citizens Capital Markets and Santander Bank served as co-underwriters, and Cantor Fitzgerald served as equity capital markets advisor. Charles Schwab CEO: Considering Entering the Prediction Market Sector (April 17) – Charles Schwab is considering entering the prediction market, but its President and CEO, Rick Wurster, believes there is a significant difference between betting on Taylor Swift's love life and betting on the latest inflation data. “At some point, we will likely launch a prediction market,” Wurster said on the company’s first-quarter earnings call on Thursday, distinguishing his bets on financial events from topics such as sports, politics, and popular culture. However, Wurster stated that Charles Schwab is not one of the companies rushing to market products related to this sector. "This isn't at the top of our client needs list, and if you look at traders' success rate data, it's not high; people usually lose money. This is definitely something we'll seriously look into, and it's quite straightforward for us to offer, and when we do, we'll make it more formal." Polkadot cross-chain bridge attacked, 1 billion DOT maliciously minted and dumped. On April 13th, according to PeckShield monitoring, 1 billion DOT tokens were abnormally minted and dumped on the Ethereum chain. On-chain data shows that about an hour earlier, attackers tampered with administrator privileges, transferring contract control to a malicious address, then immediately minted 1 billion DOT and dumped them, causing the price to plummet from $1.22 to near zero. The incident is still developing, and Polkadot has not yet issued a response. The target of this attack was a bridge asset on Ethereum, not the Polkadot native chain.

data.title: Is it illegal for cryptocurrency KOLs to create paid communities and sell courses?
data.description:
Author: Lawyer Shao Shiwei

In his daily work, Lawyer Shao encounters many cryptocurrency KOLs and those offering paid knowledge services, primarily providing instruction or sharing on "how to invest and trade in virtual currencies."

These clients often have similar questions: on the one hand, they feel that this type of business may involve certain risks; on the other hand, they see many colleagues around them continuously engaging in related businesses. This leads to confusion—is this business even viable? Are there really any legal risks?

Based on these frequently asked questions that have repeatedly arisen in past consultations, this article organizes these issues into a Q&A format, providing a systematic response to these common concerns (to protect the privacy of those seeking advice, relevant information has been anonymized; only general and representative questions have been selected for this compilation). Question: Hello Attorney Shao, I am a KOL in the cryptocurrency industry. I mainly post cryptocurrency analysis videos on domestic and international social media platforms (such as Bilibili, Xiaohongshu, YouTube, etc.), driving traffic to WeChat through these videos, and then establishing paid communities, charging community members USDT or fiat currency. The community services include providing my recorded trading courses, daily market analysis, and Q&A sessions. My question is, I'm not dealing in traditional stocks, but in cryptocurrency. The country itself doesn't recognize this, and I don't have the relevant business license. Will this make a difference? Could the lack of clear regulation actually reduce the risk? Answer: This is precisely where the complexity of the risk lies. Because there is no clear licensing system, it is difficult to directly apply the crime of "illegal business operations." However, when handling such cases, judicial organs often refer to the spirit of domestic policies related to virtual currencies as the basis for their investigations. Question: I've also heard that the regulation of virtual currencies has been strengthened, such as the Notice No. 2.6 issued this year. So, what legal risks does my current business model face? How can I make it more compliant? Answer: Based on your description of your business model, it has indeed entered a range with obvious legal risks. In practice, KOLs who create content and build communities cannot be viewed with a one-size-fits-all approach. They can be roughly divided into three levels, with significant differences in risk at each level: The first type is the pure content creator. They primarily analyze historical trends and explain basic knowledge, without providing real-time market analysis or making immediate judgments on specific market conditions. Even if they charge, they only sell pre-recorded courses. This type generally carries relatively low risk, but it doesn't mean there's no risk at all. The key is whether the content will be interpreted as providing operational guidance. The second type is the semi-guided type, which falls in the middle. They typically build paid communities, providing daily market analysis, interpretation, and Q&A. While they don't directly give specific buy or sell points, they will make judgments such as "This position is worth watching" or "There's an opportunity here." This type is the easiest to be identified as "providing investment advice" in practice, and the risks often begin to accumulate at this stage. The third type is the more typical "strong order placement" type. They will clearly provide buy and sell points, operating ranges, and even establish a certain connection with the user's trading results and profits. Once a dispute arises or is reported, this type is more likely to be directly included in the scope of criminal evaluation, and the handling will be significantly different. Specifically, in your business, the core risk lies in the fact that the content you share within the community is not just simple knowledge teaching, but includes your real-time analysis and judgment of market conditions (for example, marking on a chart "This may be a good long opportunity"). This content is likely to be considered to have the nature of "guiding trading," influencing members' decision-making behavior. Even if you emphasize trading logic rather than directly calling for orders, it is still essentially providing investment analysis advice. Offering paid investment consulting services such as market analysis, daily market analysis, and Q&A can easily be considered "trading guidance." Question: What specific legal risks would I face if my content were deemed "trading guidance"? Is it civil or criminal liability? Answer: The risks are mainly divided into two categories: 1. Civil Risk: If your advice leads to investment losses for users, they may file a civil lawsuit against you, claiming compensation for their losses. Although you haven't promised returns, and the risk is relatively controllable, litigation itself will consume time and energy. 2. **Criminal Risk:** This is more concerning. If users report losses to the police, or are maliciously reported, the police may open an investigation. Possible charges include **fraud**, **fundraising fraud**, etc. The logic is: users invest based on their trust in your advice, and after incurring losses, they may claim your actions constitute fraud. **Q:** What content might be considered "investment advice" (or "guiding trading")? Answer: Attorney Shao previously encountered a KOL who ran a knowledge-sharing community. He initially focused on "teaching," but gradually transitioned to analyzing market trends in the group daily, occasionally mentioning things like, "This position is worth watching." He maintained that he wasn't "giving trading advice." However, after users suffered losses and reported the incidents, the core issue the police ultimately determined was: Did the users make trades based on his judgment? Therefore, based on relevant judicial practices, policy guidelines, and our experience handling numerous Web3 and cryptocurrency cases, the following behaviors may be considered "investment advice" with the nature of "guiding trading," thus creating legal risks: 1. Providing specific buy and sell points. This is the most direct form of "giving trading advice"—clearly telling users when to buy and when to sell. For example, directly stating in communities or videos, "Bitcoin can be entered at this position," "Buy Ethereum at the current price, target XX," or "This is the stop-loss point." 2. Providing Market Analysis and Trend Prediction Even without directly saying "buy," providing analysis of the current market and predictions of future trends is also a high-risk behavior. For example, analyzing candlestick patterns, predicting the short-term trend of Bitcoin or a certain cryptocurrency in market analysis videos, interpreting market hotspots and hinting at opportunities, and giving market outlooks or trading ranges. 3. Teaching Methodology This is a situation that many consultants easily misunderstand—simply explaining methodology shouldn't count as guiding investment? In judicial practice, if the methodology in a paid course is essentially about teaching **coin selection or trading techniques with clear operational guidance**, and implies that clients can follow suit, it tends to be considered a disguised form of "investment advice." The reason is that this is no longer about teaching pure candlestick chart fundamentals or trading history, but rather has extremely strong **operational guidance**; users pay to learn how to make money. Users pay to join a community because they believe your "logic" can help them profit in the market. 4. Using "teaching" as a guise for "trading guidance" For courses and teaching communities, as long as their core is to provide real-time, targeted market analysis and operational advice, risks still exist.

Question:So, as long as my current service content doesn't predict the future and only talks about historical trends and trading methods, can it achieve zero risk?

Answer:The core judgment criterion is whether the content constitutes "investment advice." The key to judgment is not how you define your service as teaching or sharing experience, but whether, from the perspective of ordinary users (paying students), your course content substantially constitutes "investment advice" or "trading guidance."

Question: I mainly share mainstream cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin and don't recommend smaller coins. I also emphasize that investment involves risk and that caution is advised. Does this mean my actual risk is relatively low?

Answer: Your approach of sharing mainstream cryptocurrencies, emphasizing risk, and not promising returns does reduce risk to some extent. However, it ultimately depends on your overall style and content positioning within the community. This will determine whether you attract a relatively rational user group or users who are primarily speculative. These speculative users are more likely to take extreme measures (such as reporting to the police or whistleblowing) when they suffer losses. Q: If I explicitly state "For reference only, not investment advice" or "For educational purposes only, not investment advice" when users pay me, can I avoid criminal risks? A: No. Criminal cases are determined based on the principle of "substance over form," a thorough review process. The judicial authorities will not stop reviewing the substance of your business simply because you stated "not investment advice" in the contract. The key to judgment lies in what you actually did and what content you delivered, not what you claimed to the public. In conclusion, many people seeking advice are primarily concerned with several practical questions: Am I already crossing the line in my current situation? If I stop now, is it too late? Will the money I earned before be subject to retroactive review? There is no single answer to these questions. This needs to be analyzed in the context of specific business operations. It typically depends on several key factors: How your content is presented—is it interpreted as providing operational guidance? Do users' transaction behaviors correspond to your content? Does the business pricing model relate to users' transaction behaviors? These issues often directly influence the final evaluation in specific cases. If you are currently in a similar business phase and unsure of your risk boundaries, it's advisable not to base your judgment solely on your own understanding of "teaching" or "sharing." If you are already aware of the risks but cannot pinpoint their specific locations, you need to re-analyze the key facts. Some questions only yield truly meaningful answers when viewed within the context of the specific business structure.

data.title: Binance Life: A 15-Fold Increase to a New High – Three Lifelines Saved by an Artificial Bull Market
data.description:
CZ's new book, "Binance Life," has been released, and the token of the same name has begun a violent upward trend, reaching the high point of this round of price increases, rising 15 times from the low point at the end of March.

This is a 15-fold increase at a time when cryptocurrency trading volume is dismal, the wealth effect is shrinking sharply, Bitcoin has been halved from its peak, and simply put, the cryptocurrency market is at the bottom of the technology asset industry.

The 15x increase brought a wealth effect far greater than that of "Binance Life" alone. On Binance, altcoins saw a long-awaited 24-hour doubling in price, and familiar fund rotations began to appear, such as inscription, Chinese memes, or Polkadot-related coins. The "Binance Life" price surge slightly revitalized the crypto market, which had hit rock bottom. This happened again on Binance. This artificial bull market in a bear market has occurred twice before, precisely when it was most needed. On January 28, 2019, BTT opened for subscription on Binance Launchpad, with the window lasting 18 minutes. The $7.12 million allocation sold out in 18 minutes. The offering price was $0.00012, and eight days later it had increased more than tenfold. That was a time when everyone was lost and directionless during a super bear market. Binance Launchpad solved a very direct problem: giving ordinary investors an entry point to acquire early access to tokens. This had never happened before. If you bought it, you were one step ahead of others. The scarcity was real, and FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) arose naturally. In March 2022, BTC fell 34% from its all-time high of $68,700 to $37,800. While most investors were still undecided whether to exit or continue waiting, STEPN's governance token, GMT, completed its subscription on Binance Launchpad at an offering price of $0.01. On April 24th, GMT was trading at $4.1144, a 411-fold increase from its offering price, with a peak circulating market capitalization of $2.46 billion. Google Trends data showed that global search interest for "STEPN" reached a perfect score of 100 that week, almost coinciding with the price peak. In June, daily active users reached 1 million. Copycat projects subsequently emerged, with at least 17 Move-to-earn projects launching. Compared to BTT, GMT's narrative is on a higher level. BTT sells scarcity. GMT sells a story supported by behavioral logic: earning money by walking—explainable and shareable. It accurately captured two collective emotions in the post-pandemic era: a yearning for outdoor activities and a fantasy of crypto returns. A Binance-backed platform + a compelling product narrative = the conditions for igniting a firestorm in a bear market. Four years later, when the crypto community desperately needed something to shake things up, "Binance Life" appeared. Coupled with the release of Changpeng Zhao's new book and the launch of a nationwide reading week, old stories were reopened, generating sustained media buzz and pushing meme coins to a new all-time high. BTT needed a trading protocol, GMT needed a pair of sneakers and a mobile app. Binance Life needed only a sentence, a meme, and a book title. In terms of process, the "Binance Life" event wasn't as organic as previous ones. Veteran investors will surely remember the IEO where delivery riders would skip 8 PM to grab a share of the new coin; the "running to earn money" update directly redefined Web3 and became a trend. This time, there were basically no newcomers in the market, but coupled with a popular new book, the volatile coins on Binance brought long-awaited altcoin volatility, and in terms of results, it seems more satisfying than the previous two. This is certainly not a macro bull market; money hasn't yet flowed to Bitcoin, let alone altcoins. But even if it's a short-lived, artificial bull market, it's surprisingly moving.

data.title: Hyperliquid has become the top interest of traditional hedge funds.
data.description:
Source: Empire Podcast - Markets Bounce Back, The Hyperliquid Thesis and Kraken Raises $200M

Host:

Jason Yanowitz (Co-founder of Blockworks)

Santiago Roel Santos (Santi, crypto investor, former partner of ParaFi Capital)

Editor's Note

Santi makes a cautionary statement on the show: This is the most intense interest in the crypto industry from traditional hedge fund managers since Paul Tudor Jones called Bitcoin "the fastest horse on the track" in May 2020, and this time the focus is not on BTC, but on Hyperliquid.

The catalyst is the Iranian conflict.

Commodities need a pricing venue over the weekend when traditional markets are closed. Trade XYZ's median error in predicting weekend oil prices to Monday's opening price was only 50 basis points, which boosted Hyperliquid's commodity open interest to 17%, surpassing Ethereum. Santi predicts that commodity trading volume will exceed Bitcoin this year, and Hyperliquid's market capitalization is expected to jump from $25 billion to $100 billion. Two other key indicators: Kraken's valuation has shrunk from $20 billion at the end of 2025 to $13.3 billion; Deutsche Börse invested $200 million for a 1.5% stake (the discount may stem from the structural difference between common and preferred stock); and the SEC clarified this week that self-custodied DeFi front-ends (MetaMask, Phantom, etc.) are not bound by broker-dealer registration rules, resolving one of the biggest unresolved legal issues in the DeFi world.

Key Quotes

Hyperliquid and Weekend Markets

This is the most interest I've seen from traditional hedge fund managers in crypto since Paul Tudor Jones called Bitcoin the fastest horse on the track in May 2020, and that interest is focused on Hyperliquid. Weekend markets are a big reason for this. (Santi)

Hyperliquid essentially moves the entire CME matching engine, order book, clearinghouse, and margin system onto an integrated blockchain, with every transaction, clearing, and funding fee recorded in an immutable public ledger. (Santi)

My prediction: Commodity trading volume on Hyperliquid will surpass Bitcoin's this year.

It currently has a market capitalization of $25 billion, and I believe there's a clear path to $100 billion. (Santi) I give it a 30% to 40% chance of obtaining US compliance approval. If it does get approved, it will be an instant re-rating. CME's market capitalization is $117 billion, and the market always needs a benchmark. (Jason) Hyperliquid's Institutional Adoption Path I received a call this week from a large hedge fund manager asking me to explain Hyperliquid to him. Their team spends most of their time on Twitter, and their trading inspiration also comes from Twitter. Since their fund can't directly buy HYPE tokens, they bought Hyperliquid's DAT (Digital Asset Treasury), which is now one of their largest holdings. He said this was one of the few assets he could buy that wasn't related to AI trading. (Jason) Trading Philosophy and Personal Positions My core logic for continuously adding to my positions is based on my own consumption patterns. The companies where I spend the most money year after year (Amazon, DoorDash) are the targets I add to my positions in. A senior colleague once said that the younger you are, the more alpha you can gain from being "close to the front lines," and in the future, there will be less and less alpha on the internet; you have to discover it by seeing it with your own eyes and using it yourself. (Jason) The Link Between Macro and Crypto The past 10 trading days have been one of the most unusual ten days since 1950. The S&P 500 rose 9.8%, placing it at the 99.7th percentile of all historical 10-day gains. (Santi) I think the strong performance of crypto is due to a good macro environment and a good stock market; the correlation is very strong. Bitcoin hit $75,000 mainly because the market bet that the probability of the Clarity Act passing has increased from 50/50 last week to 60% to 65% now. (Jason) Regulatory Shift The SEC told MetaMask, Phantom, and basically all DeFi front-ends this week: You are not brokers. This ends one of the biggest legal mysteries in DeFi. (Jason) L2 and the "Melting Ice Block" The L2 sector has been like a melting ice block for the past year or so. In 2017, you could sell dreams even without users or data; today, the crypto market's patience for white paper-style narratives is almost zero. (Santi) I think this round will really see a batch of protocols go to zero, not just fading into oblivion like in the past, but the market simply abandoning them. Funds will only concentrate on truly successful assets like Hyperliquid and Bitcoin. (Santi) Next Round of Opportunities My three main investment themes are: demand for stablecoins outside the US will continue to expand and is far from being met; the world will only become more volatile; and there is a serious global affordability crisis. These three pillars all have corresponding implementation paths on-chain. (Santi) Last year's three hottest topics were derivatives, prediction markets, and stablecoins. Projects doing these three things were able to secure funding and users, while projects outside these areas were crushed. This year there will be three new things, but I don't know what they are yet. (Jason) I guess one of the three things that happened this year was tokenization, especially the attempts made by traditional issuers on public permissionless networks. (Santi) III. Full Dialogue Opening: Podcast Data Review and Content Differentiation in the AI ​​Era Santi: This is the most interest I've seen from traditional hedge fund managers in crypto since Paul Tudor Jones (a well-known macro hedge fund manager) called Bitcoin "the fastest horse on the track" in May 2020, and that interest is focused on Hyperliquid (an on-chain perpetual contract exchange). The weekend market was a big reason for that. Jason: Hello listeners, welcome back to Empire Weekly. Santi, how have you been lately? Santi: Nothing special, just happy to be back on the show. A lot is happening in the crypto market and the world at large; it's good to live in the moment. Jason: Santi's been stealing our producer's job lately. He imported all the Empire data into Claude, using the new Opus 4.7 model, and asked, "How can we make Empire better?" Santi: Not entirely, but I did do it. I exported data from Megaphone and YouTube, went through all the shows since 2021, and ran correlation analyses with crypto asset prices. We often say "views are linked to crypto prices," but the actual data shows a lower correlation. Jason: So, not that correlated? Santi: Yes. Crypto content generally falls into two categories: one is purely about price, which goes viral on YouTube; our in-depth interview-style content performs better on Spotify and Apple, but not on YouTube. Retail investors on YouTube gain followers fastest during bull markets; they follow the market trends. Jason: YouTube's algorithm will penalize us. TLDR means we need to create more sensational headlines, but that's not our brand image. I've talked to Brian and David from Bankless many times about this; their thumbnails are very exaggerated. But I'm too proud; there's a line I'll never cross: I won't create content like BitBoy. Santi: Respect their path. Jason: Never become BitBoy, and never become Joe McCann (a prominent figure in the crypto space). Okay, let's get to the point. Market Overview: The Two Sides of the S&P 500's 10-Day Rally and the AI ​​Narrative Santi: Wait, let me talk about the market first. Two weeks ago we were discussing a 10% market downturn, and now the Nasdaq has just completed a 10-day winning streak. Jason: Yes, that's historically very rare. I looked up the data, and a 10-day winning streak only happens about once every 5 to 7 years on average. Since the dot-com bubble in 2000, it's only happened a few times. Santi: To be honest, the past 10 trading days have been one of the most unusual ten days since 1950. The S&P 500 rose 9.8%, placing it at the 99.7th percentile of all historical 10-day gains. Jason: I've been thinking about this today: Just stay optimistic. If you're optimistic, you're in the right place. I don't know how this round will end, whether it will end, or if this is a new paradigm, but the data is there and cannot be ignored. Last week, we saw a great in-depth report on Hyperliquid in Colossus (an interview magazine founded by Patrick O'Shaughnessy). On a macro level, I'm willing to change my mind at any time. I recently listened to a 4-hour podcast by Leopold Aschenbrenner (former OpenAI researcher, now managing a hedge fund). This guy was right when he was fired from OpenAI two years ago. For the first hour or two of the podcast, he was discussing how we'll reach AGI sooner than most people expect, and if it falls into the hands of different regimes, it's a nuclear arms race-level risk. He advocates for the nationalization of AI labs and opposes open source. You could hear the tension in his tone. He felt that very few people truly understood how fast and far this could go, and the market wasn't taking it seriously enough. Santi: Was that the "Situational Awareness" article he wrote two years ago? Jason: Yes. I tweeted, "I finally finished reading Leopold's article on AI. You should read it whether you're interested in AI or not. These are my notes." That tweet went viral, with over 10,000 saves and 1.6 million views. But my mistake was only taking notes; I didn't buy anything. Santi: He bought some himself. How big is his fund now? Jason: I don't know the initial size, but it's $5.5 billion now. He changes his portfolio frequently. His largest holdings now include Bloom Energy (a hydrogen energy company), where he invested $300 million, now worth $1 billion; and a large position in SanDisk (memory chips). By the way, his partner is Anthropic CEO Dario's Chief of Staff. Santi: That's quite a bit of information. Before working at a hedge fund and joining OpenAI, he also worked at the Future Fund, a project funded by Sam Bankman-Fried of FTX. In that podcast episode, he talked about his experience working with SBF and the lessons he learned, which was quite interesting. Jason: Here's some data about FTX: SBF held 7.8% of Anthropic at the time. Did you see the news this week? Anthropic's new valuation is $800 billion. FTX's stake would be worth more than the entire market capitalization of Coinbase. Santi: Yes, and it ended up being liquidated by the custodian, for about $1.5 billion. Jason: Crazy. FTX creditors ultimately received their full payments, even with a premium. Public market investors are very nervous now, always looking for something. There's both fear and excitement surrounding AI, a coin with two sides. This sentiment brings dramatic volatility and pullbacks, but the market fundamentally wants to go up. Everyone realizes this is an out-of-control train, unstoppable. Santi: On one hand, no one knows how to model the positive and negative impacts of AI into valuations. These labs are releasing one groundbreaking technology after another, and not being able to keep up creates tension. On the other hand, after listening to Leopold, Elon, and Dario's podcasts, you get a sense of awe that "we are at the tipping point of human civilization." Rockets fly into outer space and come back; the market is both nervous and willing to move upwards. Trading Philosophy: Finding Alpha Through Consumption Habits Jason: Could you elaborate on your position? Santi: I tweeted about Situational Awareness, and haven't sold anything since. I'm just a bag holder; I buy and hold. I'm not a very interesting podcast guest because I don't trade much. I took Robinhood from $10 to $15 and all the way to $125, and I'm still adding to my position. DoorDash is the same; the company I spend the most money on year after year is the one I keep adding to. Jason: That's really clever logic. I always remember what you said before: look at your annual spending patterns; there are some companies you spend more and more on each year. Amazon is one, and DoorDash is another. Santi: I took a class in my freshman year where a friend's father was a guest speaker. He was managing a hedge fund of about $5 billion to $10 billion at the time. He said all the best trades of his career came from observing his and his children's spending. His daughter said, "I want an iPhone," so he bought an Apple product; his daughter said, "My friends are all using Snapchat," so he participated in Snapchat's early funding round. He told us 18- and 19-year-olds: You don't realize it, being close to the front lines is alpha. The older you get, the harder it is to acquire alpha. He also said: We are moving towards a world without alpha on the internet; you have to see it with your own eyes and use it yourself to discover it. Jason: Reddit used to be full of alpha. There was a hedge fund that made money by scraping Reddit. Before the release of the Barbie movie, he read Reddit reviews and found that "this movie is a masterpiece," while Rotten Tomatoes gave it a low score. He then heavily invested in Mattel (the manufacturer of Barbie) and made a fortune. So you can create a Claude instance and have it scrape all sorts of information from Reddit.

Hyperliquid In-Depth Analysis: Wall Street's New Darling

Santi: Okay, let's get to the news.

Jason: There's so much to talk about: Tether's endorsement of Drift's (a perpetual contract protocol on Solana) user relief plan; Tether launching a new wallet—these are two news items about Tether; Rob's VC manifesto; Kraken raising $200 million and preparing for an IPO; and Hyperliquid's in-depth coverage on Colossus. Let's start with Hyperliquid.

Santi: Let me first introduce Colossus. Patrick O'Shaughnessy runs the Invest Like the Best podcast and Positive Sum asset management company. He launched a new print and digital magazine called Colossus, which specializes in in-depth feature articles on tech finance figures, and the quality is top-notch globally.

This time, they wrote about the story of Hyperliquid founder Jeff Yan. Jason: Let me steal a point from you. You said something very apt in our episode with Logan Jastremski: it's been a long time since I've felt this much interest in a crypto project from outside the crypto space. Just like Polymarket's explosive growth due to the presidential election controversy, Jeff has now captured Wall Street's imagination. The geopolitical catalyst this time is everyone wanting to trade commodities over the weekend, and Hyperliquid caught that wave of traffic. Santi: I think even if the situation between Iran and the Strait of Hormuz eases, this trend won't stop. The train has already started moving; you'll see more trading volume and activity pouring in. Currently, the trading volume of non-crypto assets on Hyperliquid has already surpassed that of crypto assets. Jason: Really? Santi: Commodities are now Hyperliquid's second-largest asset class, second only to Bitcoin. I'll make a predictable one: commodities will surpass Bitcoin this year. Hyperliquid could become one of the top five crypto companies by market capitalization. It's currently $25 billion, and I think there's a clear path to $100 billion. Coinbase and Robinhood are in that category. If Hyperliquid continues to execute well, they have the technical talent, they've already won over crypto traders, and now they're winning over Wall Street traders. Jason: Here are the numbers. Bitcoin accounts for 25% of Hyperliquid's holdings, commodities 17%, Ethereum 16%, HYPE tokens themselves 10%, index tokens (likely leveraged S&P 500) 7%, L1 tokens 4%, stocks 4%, Solana 3.5%, followed by meme coins, privacy coins, and DeFi. Santi: I think the most interesting story in the crypto world right now is probably the growth of the oil market on Hyperliquid. This is thanks to Trade XYZ (the derivatives front-end on the Hyperliquid ecosystem) created by the Shoku team. When the situation with Iran broke out, trading volume and open interest exploded because the oil market was closed for the weekend, and Hyperliquid became the only pricing venue over the weekend. This thing crossed the mainstream divide. Jason: We specifically built a website called Weekend Markets (weekendmarkets.com) to display all this data. When traditional markets are closed, Trade XYZ is the only active price discovery venue. Weekend trading volume is in the billions of dollars, and the median error in predicting Monday's opening price is only 50 basis points. Now, Trade XYZ prices are the most informative signal in the entire financial market relative to Friday's closing price. The website shows all traded stocks and commodities, charts, heatmaps, and methodologies. Traditional institutional investors are now likely more interested in Hyperliquid's weekend trading than anything else in crypto. Santi: Oil daily trading volume is $500 million, with open interest at $350 million. Jason: People outside the crypto world often ask you questions. Are they primarily interested in commodities or stocks? Santi: The most interesting thing is Hyperliquid itself. I'd say this is the biggest interest traditional hedge fund managers have in crypto since Paul Tudor Jones called Bitcoin "the fastest horse" in May 2020—in six years. This time, the interest is in Hyperliquid, with weekend trading being the core driver. I received a call this week from a hedge fund manager asking me to explain Hyperliquid to him. His fund is very large, and he and his team spend most of their time on Twitter, getting their trading inspiration from Twitter as well. This incident perfectly illustrates the importance of Twitter. He said a top trader he knew tweeted about Hyperliquid, and he followed up by listening to every podcast Jeff appeared on. Their fund couldn't directly buy HYPE tokens, so they bought Hyperliquid's DAT (Digital Asset Treasury), which happened to be DAT's bull case. Now it's one of their largest positions, and he said it's one of the few assets he could buy that isn't related to AI trading. Jason: I just had AI explain Hyperliquid to TradFi: "Imagine CME moving its matching engine, order book, clearinghouse, and margin system all onto an integrated blockchain, with every transaction, clearing, and funding fee recorded in an immutable public ledger. That's Hyperliquid." Hyperliquid Compliance Prospects: CME is the Best Benchmark. Santi: Is it possible that once Hyperliquid is approved for compliance in the US, it will undergo a large-scale revaluation? Jason: I'm neither a lawyer nor a regulator, so this is beyond my expertise. But Hyperliquid did hire Jake Chervinsky (former Chief Legal Officer of Variant Fund) to head the Hyperliquid Policy Institute. The logic is this: Following the 2008 financial crisis, the Dodd-Frank Act required futures trading through regulated entities like CME and Cboe, aiming for transparency and making all transactions visible to prevent black swan events. If you were Hyperliquid or Jake, your argument to the CFTC would be: you created this rule precisely for transparency, to prevent further problems. We have DeFi, where all transactions are completely transparent, and the clearing mechanism largely passed the stress test on October 10th. If they haven't argued this way, they should. Imagine Hyperliquid being approved in the US; it would instantly revalue. CME currently has a market capitalization of $117 billion; the market always needs a benchmark. Santi: I'd give it about a 30% to 40% probability. Jason: Have you talked to hedge funds and heard any negative opinions about Hyperliquid? Santi: Not much. Most people are just starting to understand and haven't formed a clear bullish or bearish view yet. Don't forget that Solana's peak protocol revenue also reached $100 million, mostly from meme coin trading. Jason: The peak of REV (Real Economic Value). Santi: Yes, in January 2024 or 2025, Solana reached $500 million in REV, almost all of it in meme coins. Now the question is: should the market give the protocol revenue generated by meme coin trading the same valuation multiple as the protocol revenue generated by TradFi commodity trading? Jason: Of course not, commodity revenue is of higher quality. Santi: So I think Hyperliquid can achieve a much larger scale than that. Jason: I saw some data: the protocols most mentioned by all speakers at DAS (Digital Asset Summit, a summit hosted by Blockworks). We crawled all the panels, fireside sessions, and keynotes. Bitcoin was mentioned 742 times, and Ethereum 143 times—those two are definitely outliers. Third is Hyperliquid with 66 times, Morpho (a lending protocol) with 59 times, followed by Solana, Canton, Espresso (a shared ordering protocol), Circle, Stellar, Avalanche, Jupiter, Tether, Maple, Optimism, and Superstate. Espresso ranking 7th is quite unexpected. Drift's bailout plan and Tether's "killing two birds with one stone" Jason: What other news did you follow this week? Santi: Tether invested in Drift to help protocol users recover their losses (Drift suffered a vulnerability attack earlier this year that affected approximately $285 million). This approach is similar to Bitfinex's strategy of issuing tokens to compensate victims, with affected users gradually recovering their losses through future transaction fees. This time, Tether is providing up to approximately $127 million in support, bringing the total to nearly $150 million. Jason: The specific mechanism requires users to trade with USDT on Drift to recover their losses. This is a win-win for Tether, as it both does public relations and locks in the use of stablecoins. Many people on Twitter are comparing it to USDC; reportedly, someone contacted USDC to freeze hacker funds, but Circle's response was not as quick as Tether's. This is a loss for Circle. Santi: This is a clever mechanism that incentivizes users to continue using the protocol. To some extent, this is the only viable solution; another option is to dilute the protocol through large-scale issuance at the underlying level.

SEC's Major Guidance and Clarity Bill Progress

Jason: There's also new guidance from the SEC. They stated this week that self-custodied DeFi front-ends are not subject to broker-dealer registration rules. Previously, a major attack on DeFi during the Gensler era required these protocols to register as broker-dealers, which fundamentally contradicts the permissionless nature of DeFi and is practically impossible. On Tuesday, the SEC's Division of Trading and Markets issued a staff statement excluding crypto wallet interfaces from broker-dealer registration. The SEC explicitly told MetaMask, Phantom, and virtually all DeFi front-ends: you are not brokers. This ends one of the biggest legal mysteries surrounding DeFi. They created a new category called "covered user interface": if you help users translate transaction parameters into on-chain instructions, you belong to this category. Custodied wallets are excluded, but self-custodied wallets, browser plugins, mobile applications, and software embedded with self-custodied wallets are protected.

Santi: I'm surprised DeFi tokens haven't been revalued as a result. Jason: Robinhood was the one that revalued due to regulatory benefits; they lowered the minimum $25,000 equity requirement for day traders. Overall, Aave rose 20% this week, and Bitcoin hit $75,000. I think this is mainly due to the increased probability of the Clarity Act (crypto market structure legislation) passing. Last week you asked Empire listeners if Clarity would pass, and everyone said 50/50. I was in Washington, D.C. last week, and everyone said 50/50. Now it's probably around 60/40 or even 65/35. A few crypto celebrities went on CNBC and said, "If Clarity doesn't pass before April 1st, we're completely dead," and for some reason, that statement has been accepted. But from what I understand, even if it's delayed until May 1st, or even August, or 2027, there's still a chance. Of course, the longer it drags on, the more garbage clauses will be crammed into the bill. Some say that if it's delayed until mid-2027, it will become the "DeFi Act" (meaning it will be heavily modified by interest groups). MicroStrategy's increased holdings and the 11.5% return on STRC. Santi: I tend to think that the good performance of crypto is due to a good macro environment, and the correlation is very strong. Regarding Bitcoin, MicroStrategy raised money this week through STRC (Perpetual Preferred Stock) to buy nearly 14,000 Bitcoins. Jason: Total holdings are 780,000 Bitcoins, total investment is close to $60 billion, and the average cost is $75,000. This is one of their largest acquisitions. Now, besides looking at mNAV premiums or discounts, the market is also watching STRC's dynamics. Santi: There's a protocol called Saturn whose TVL (Total Value Locked) is growing rapidly. Essentially, it packages STRC onto the blockchain, offering an annualized return of approximately 11.5%. Jason: I went to MicroStrategy's office in Tysons Corner, Virginia on Thursday, and the entire meeting was about STRC. STRC's annualized return of 11.5% is very attractive given the generally low yields in on-chain DeFi. I won't discuss the risks today; I'll leave that for later. We should invite Saylor or someone from MicroStrategy to the show to talk about it. What everyone is concerned about is "where does this 11.5% return come from, its sustainability, and what are the risks?" Kraken's Valuation Halved and a Breakdown of the Exchange's Business Model Santi: Speaking of Kraken, they took $200 million from Deutsche Börse. Jason: The valuation dropped from $20 billion at the end of 2025 to $13.3 billion, which is a significant discount. Santi: I haven't seen that valuation figure. It's probably a secondary market transaction; they bought common stock instead of preferred stock, hence the structural difference. Jason: Yes, Deutsche Börse bought 1.5% of the shares at a valuation of $13.3 billion. My guess is that because it's common stock, there was a discount of around 35%. Additionally, Kraken experienced a small-scale security incident, unrelated to user funds, which was an internal issue within the support team. I'll mention it as a footnote. However, Kraken is a very well-run company with the potential to reach Coinbase's scale. Arjun (Arjun Sethi, Kraken's co-CEO) is doing a great job. I might look into secondary market opportunities. Santi: I invested in Backpack (an exchange founded by Armani Ferrante), which was much smaller but very interesting. Armani is an excellent builder. The core issue in evaluating an exchange's business model is the ratio of fixed to variable costs. Exchanges are essentially tied to crypto prices; they are very profitable when prices are high, but their profitability is compressed during downturns. I previously conducted a comparative analysis of Coinbase, Robinhood, and Kraken. Robinhood was the best, with diversified business (a high proportion of non-crypto trading volume) and the ability to significantly reduce costs during bear markets. Coinbase was second, and Kraken was third. But that was an analysis before Arjun took over. They later restructured the team, bringing in many capable operators, and the DeFi team is doing very well. So perhaps my previous assessment was too conservative. Any business needs to consider operating leverage and resilience; in a bear market, profit margins cannot be crushed—this is the perspective of a cross-cycle investor. Jason: What about X Stocks? I've seen some discussions about pre-IPO stocks on Twitter, such as Anthropic. Santi: The trading volume is quite small, not as dramatic as oil. Jason: I have data; currently, pre-IPO related stock trading is around $17 million, which is still very small. I spoke with the X Stocks team on Kraken a few days ago, and they, along with other exchanges, believe that X Stocks will have a major breakthrough this year. The X Stocks team and others at Kraken are betting on this direction. Here's a tweet from Nikita, the X (Twitter) product manager: "X has always been the best source of financial information for traders and investors, with billions of dollars flowing daily into timeline-based content. Today we're launching the cashtag feature on iPhones in the US and Canada, bringing real-time financial data. When you search for or post a cashtag or contract address, X automatically matches stocks or tokens, and clicking on a cashtag shows price movements and related posts—all without leaving X." This is Elon's everything-app vision in progress. Scroll: Controversy and the "Ice Block Effect" of the L2 Track Santi: How's the Ethereum ecosystem lately? Besides Tom Lee saying ETH will reach $62,000. Jason: I don't know, I'm not that in-depth. The ECC (Ethereum Community Conference) just ended; the atmosphere was so-so, but there were still some interesting projects discussed. Santi: Ether.fi migrated from Scroll to the Optimism mainnet, migrating over 70,000 cards. Jason: You're an investor in Scroll, so I don't know how much you can talk about. Scroll seems to have done something rather unethical. When Ether.fi wanted to migrate from Scroll to Optimism, they manually increased the on-chain gas fees. One of Ether.fi's founders tweeted, "This is even more outrageous than people think." Santi: For context: Ether.fi is crucial to Scroll; if Ether.fi were separated, Scroll would have very little TVL. I don't have firsthand information about what happened internally. The publicly available on-chain data shows that in the first six days of April, Scroll increased the fees for publishing data on the Ethereum mainnet by 1000 times, two orders of magnitude. Users ended up paying over $50,000 more in fees as a result. The timing is indeed suspicious, coinciding with the Ether.fi migration window. The on-chain data is there, and it looks pretty bad. Jason: What went wrong with Scroll? Santi: I was an early investor. At the time, ZK-rollups and zero-knowledge proofs were hot, and I thought this approach itself had value and meaning. Scroll's co-founder was a deep cryptographer, so I bet on it. But it wasn't just Scroll; many projects in the entire L2 space faded into obscurity. Can you name a single L2 project that performed well? Vitalik's article about the L2 roadmap earlier this year was itself an admission; the market had already noticed this. The price performance of Optimism, Arbitrum, ZKsync, etc., all illustrate this point. Scroll is currently valued at $8 million, with a fully diluted valuation of $45 million, lower than its initial funding round valuation. That's a terrible situation. Jason: Can it come back? What does it need? Where will the trading volume come from? Santi: This reminds me of something I've talked about with many infrastructure providers: they are melting ice, whether they want to admit it or not. In 2017, without users and data, you could sell your dreams. Any founder, Elon or Scroll, had to sell their dreams before cashing in. The crypto world has long been in a "suspension period" of white papers and experiments, and the market has been patient with that. Not today. A year ago, I said there would be a serious "property owners vs. propertyless" divide in the crypto world, and today that divide is already very large; you can see this by comparing Hyperliquid's activity level with other projects. Three Investment Themes and the New Cycle of "Protocol True Zeroing" Santi: The neobank category is quite popular recently. Plasma (a dedicated chain for stablecoins), Ether.fi are all working on it, and Aave also has its own neobank attempt. As an investor, I have three main themes, which are also reflected in my personal holdings: 1. The demand for stablecoins outside the US will continue to expand and is far from being met; 2. The world will only become more volatile; 3. There is a serious global affordability crisis. These three pillars all have on-chain implementation paths, and the investment strategy should follow this line of thinking. However, most other projects face difficulties. Solana's REV also plummeted from a peak of $500 million to $20 million. What can revive Solana? There are some positive aspects at the core: Western Union chose Solana as a pilot, and the team and engineers are very capable, genuinely thinking about the problem. However, in my conversations with these teams, I feel they must go for the jugular, using capital or unconventional methods to cement themselves as a persistent protocol. Finally, I want to say that in this round, I believe some protocols will truly go to zero, not just settle into dust for the next round. The old approach was "hold dust, dust will become the moon," like Lend merging into Aave. This round won't be so widespread. The market prefers to concentrate funds in Hyperliquid and Bitcoin. The three major narratives of the next round and RWA trends. Jason: Besides Hyperliquid and Bitcoin, what else might ordinary people be interested in or have heard of when you talk to them? Santi: Stablecoins and prediction markets are hot topics, but prediction markets currently don't have tokens. Jason: These things take time to gain widespread public awareness. We've always said that last year's three hot topics were perpetual contracts, prediction markets, and stablecoins. Projects doing these three things were able to secure funding and users, while projects outside these areas were crushed. This year, there will be three new things, but I don't know what they are. Santi: Will it? Jason: I guess it will. The term tokenization (stocks on-chain, RWA real-world assets) has been overused, but it will be one of the three hot topics this year.
